subject_profile.summary: Base country-level assessment; default category weights prioritize rule of law, institutional integrity, and personal freedom.
stated_values: Rule of law and institutional integrity weighted most heavily
Personal freedom and security from arbitrary state action are foundational
Economic success is contingent on rule of law, not a substitute for it

flags: Generated 2026-05-28 under v2.0: multilateral + independent (IMF, World Bank, ICJ, Transparency International, Freedom House) + international and Guyanese press; reflects Maduro's January 2026 capture by US forces, which analysts read as putting the Essequibo claim on ice near-term.
The world's fastest-growing economy - an Exxon-led offshore oil boom (~19% GDP growth, output scaling past ~900k bbl/d toward ~1.7m by 2030) transforming a once-poor nation of ~0.8m.

Standout features: extraordinary transformational oil upside almost exactly counterbalanced by severe downside tails - weak institutions/resource-curse risk, ethnic polarization, an existentially below-sea-level coast, and the live Essequibo territorial dispute with Venezuela (before the ICJ).

economic.notes: A colossal oil windfall and sovereign fund, with absorptive-capacity and Dutch-disease risk the flipside.

fiscal_state.notes: Surging oil revenue and a Natural Resource Fund (~US$2.57bn inflow 2024) underwrite expansionary budgets; the risk is spending discipline/absorptive capacity, not solvency. (IMF 2025 Art IV)

monetary_independence.notes: The Bank of Guyana runs a managed GYD kept stable by ample oil FX, with a limited toolkit and Dutch-disease appreciation pressure. (Bank of Guyana)

reserve_currency_intl_monetary.notes: The GYD is not a reserve currency. (definitional)

inflation.notes: Moderate (~2.7% in 2025) despite the boom, helped by a stable managed rate. (central-bank data)

banking_stability.notes: Liquid and growing with the oil influx, but a small, shallow system facing rapid-credit-growth and overheating risks. (IMF 2025)

trade_actions_capacity.notes: Oil exports drive a massive external surplus and reserve build; the flipside is extreme single-commodity concentration and Dutch-disease risk to non-oil tradables. (IMF; CFR)

institutional.notes: The core long-run risk - documented elite state-capture, weak enforcement, and thin absorptive capacity.

rule_of_law.notes: TI flags 'state capture by elites', illicit enrichment, and attacks on activists/journalists; anti-corruption institutions exist but enforcement is weak (CPI ~39/100). (TI 2024; Stabroek News)

statistical_integrity.notes: Usable IMF-engaged data, but capacity is thin and oil-era data systems are still maturing. (IMF; Bureau of Statistics)

civil_service_capacity.notes: A small bureaucracy badly overstretched by the speed of the boom - weak absorptive/project-delivery capacity is a recurring IMF theme. (IMF 2025)

press_freedom.notes: A functioning press, but TI/observers cite rising intimidation of journalists and civil society. (Transparency International)

political_social.notes: Orderly elections amid single-party dominance and deep ethnic polarization, with enormous per-head wealth.

political_stability.notes: Ali (PPP/C) re-elected with a landslide (~55%) in Sept 2025 - orderly, but single-party dominance and the rise of US-sanctioned Mohamed's WIN add uncertainty. (Al Jazeera; Americas Quarterly)

civil_liberties.notes: Broadly free elections and expression, but institutional weakness and elite capture constrain accountability. (Freedom House; TI)

treatment_non_citizens.notes: Hosts Venezuelan migrants and an inflowing oil-expat workforce, with no major systematic abuse reported. (inference)

social_cohesion.notes: Deep Indo-/Afro-Guyanese ethnic-political polarization (PPP vs APNU) is the historic fault line, with WIN's cross-ethnic showing a tentative softening. (Americas Quarterly)

demographics.notes: A young median age and a tiny population (~0.8m) mean oil wealth per head is enormous, tempered by an emigration history and ~68y life expectancy. (UNFPA; Worldometer)

geopolitical.notes: Deepening US/UK/CARICOM backing, against the acute Essequibo territorial dispute.

alliance_reliability.notes: Tight, deepening US/UK/CARICOM/Exxon backing on the Essequibo dispute - a US security partner of growing strategic value. (CNBC; CARICOM)

conflict_involvement.notes: The acute Essequibo dispute (two-thirds of Guyana's landmass) - Venezuela's 2023-2025 annexation moves drew ICJ provisional measures; Maduro's Jan 2026 capture by US forces eased the near-term threat, but a binding ICJ judgment is pending and post-Maduro Venezuela is uncertain - a live, unresolved tail risk. (ICJ; CNBC)

sanctions_capital_controls.notes: Guyana itself is unsanctioned with an open, oil-fuelled FX position; the tail risk is regional spillover and the sanctioned profile of the new opposition figure. (IMF; Americas Quarterly)

physical_practical.notes: An existentially below-sea-level coast, with oil-funded health/infrastructure build-out and low crime.

climate.notes: ~90% of the population and most activity sit on a coastal belt 1-3m below sea level, with local sea-level rise above the global average - existential flood/inundation exposure. (World Bank)

healthcare.notes: Historically weak (shortages, thin specialist care), but very large oil-funded investment (a Mount Sinai partnership) targets rapid upgrade by 2030. (Guyana DPI; Mount Sinai)

infrastructure.notes: Long under-built (roads, power, drainage), now in a massive oil-funded build-out (sea defences, the Brazil road link), with execution/absorption risk. (World Bank; IMF)

crime_safety.notes: Comparatively low and falling - homicide ~13.6-15.6/100k in 2025, the lowest serious-crime rate in a decade, mostly interpersonal. (InSight Crime; Bureau of Statistics)

disaster_insurance.notes: Heavy flood/sea-level catastrophe exposure on a thin domestic insurance base, despite World-Bank-financed coastal resilience. (World Bank)

summary: The world's fastest-growing economy - ~19% real GDP growth in 2025 on an Exxon-led offshore oil boom scaling past ~900k bbl/d toward ~1.7m by 2030, transforming a once-poor nation of ~0.8m people with surging fiscal revenue, a sovereign Natural Resource Fund, low inflation, a stable managed currency, and comparatively low crime. The boom is genuine and transformational, but the downside tails are equally large: weak institutions with documented 'state-capture' and corruption concerns (TI CPI ~39/100), thin civil-service absorptive capacity, deep Indo-/Afro-Guyanese ethnic polarization, an existentially exposed below-sea-level coastline, and above all the Essequibo territorial dispute with Venezuela, which escalated sharply through 2023-2025 and is now before the ICJ. Net symmetric: rarely is such enormous upside paired with such concentrated existential risk.
